planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–7.0) soil in partial shade when young, transitioning to full sun at maturity; plant at least two different pawpaw cultivars for cross-pollination — single trees fruit poorly.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade when young (seedlings require shade protection) transitioning to full sun at fruiting age; Shenandoah is one of the finest named pawpaw cultivars — selected by Kirk Pomper at Kentucky State University for its large, sweet, beautiful fruit with vanilla-custard flavor and minimal strong aftertaste that characterizes some inferior seedling-grown trees. Pawpaw (Asimina triloba) is the largest tree fruit native to North America, producing fruits 3–6 inches long with yellow-green skin, creamy-yellow flesh, and a unique tropical flavor reminiscent of banana, mango, and vanilla custard combined — sometimes called 'the custard apple of the North.' Shenandoah was specifically selected for mild, sweet flavor without the bitterness or sourness that reduces the palatability of some pawpaw seedlings. The large, heavy fruits ripen in September–October in zone 7–8 and must be picked when they yield slightly to pressure — they do not ripen off the tree well and have a very short shelf life (2–3 days at room temperature, 2–3 weeks refrigerated). Pawpaws require cross-pollination from a different named cultivar; two or more plants are needed for reliable fruit production. The flowers have a faint unpleasant odor to attract fly and beetle pollinators — supplemental hand pollination or adding flowering road-killed meat near the trees during bloom significantly improves fruit set. In zone 9, pawpaw performs in the northern portions; the deep South's heat and lack of winter chill can limit performance.
sources: Kentucky State University Pawpaw Research Program

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade when young; Sunflower is self-fertile to a degree but performs much better when cross-pollinated by another cultivar such as Shenandoah, Wells, or Susquehanna.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade when young, full sun at maturity; Sunflower is a named pawpaw cultivar selected in Ohio from wild seedlings notable for its large, somewhat self-fertile nature (better fruit set when grown alone than most pawpaw cultivars), medium-large fruit size, and rich, sweet, yellow-gold flesh with excellent banana-custard flavor. The 'sunflower' name refers to both the yellow-gold flesh color and the cultivar's tendency to produce better solo fruit set than most pawpaws, though production is still significantly improved with a cross-pollinator. Sunflower's partial self-fertility is its most important practical attribute for gardeners who have space for only one tree — it will produce some fruit without a pollinator while most other pawpaw cultivars produce little or none. The medium-to-large fruits (4–6 oz average) have green to yellowish-green skin that softens slightly and turns more yellow-toned when fully ripe; the rich, sweet, banana-custard flesh is among the most popular of named pawpaw selections. Harvest by feel rather than appearance — ripe pawpaws yield to gentle pressure like a ripe peach. As with all pawpaws, the very short post-harvest shelf life (2–5 days at room temperature) makes them a specialty fruit for home gardeners and local direct-sale; they cannot be commercially distributed through standard grocery supply chains.
sources: Kentucky State University Pawpaw Research Program

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, deep, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), fertile loam in full sun; Pawnee is Type II for pollination scheduling (sheds pollen after the female flowers are receptive) — plant with a Type I cultivar (Desirable, Cape Fear) for best cross-pollination.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, deep, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile loam in full sun; Pawnee is one of the finest pecan cultivars for the Gulf South — a USDA Agricultural Research Service introduction that has become one of the most widely planted pecans in the region for its combination of outstanding nut quality, large nut size, excellent kernel percentage (55–57%), early maturity, and partial scab resistance. Pawnee's most significant attribute is its very early maturity: it ripens 2–3 weeks earlier than Desirable, Stuart, and other standard cultivars, meaning Pawnee nuts are falling in late September to early October in zone 9 — well ahead of the alternating wet/dry weather cycles of mid-fall that cause many pecan cultivars to develop scab and quality losses. The large, thin-shelled nuts have excellent quality with bright, sweet-tasting kernels and very good percentage fill. Pawnee is a Type II pecan (protandrous pollen shedding) — it produces pollen when its own female flowers are not yet receptive, requiring cross-pollination from a Type I cultivar (like Desirable) for maximum yields. This cross-pollination requirement is easy to meet in the Gulf South where multiple pecan cultivars are commonly grown in close proximity. Pawnee is moderately susceptible to pecan scab — preventive fungicide applications during leaf-out and through spring are important in humid Gulf South conditions.

common_name: Jumbo Muscadine (Large Black Self-Fertile Muscadine Grape)

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root vines in late winter or spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun on a sturdy trellis or arbor; Jumbo is self-fertile and does not require a pollinator, making it practical for single-vine plantings.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun on a sturdy trellis or arbor; Jumbo is one of the largest-berried muscadine grape cultivars available — producing extra-large, 1–1.5 inch diameter, dark black to purplish-black berries that are significantly larger than standard muscadines and create a dramatic visual impression both on the vine and at harvest. The large size makes Jumbo berries outstanding for fresh eating, where the large, sweet, musky flesh provides a more substantial bite than smaller cultivars. The flavor is rich, sweet, and distinctly muscadine with the characteristic musky, fruity flavor that distinguishes muscadine grapes from bunch grapes. Jumbo ripens in mid to late season (September in zone 9) and is self-fertile — producing good crops without a pollinator. The vine is vigorous, productive, and well-adapted to Gulf South conditions including clay soil, heat, humidity, and the fungal disease pressure that affects European grapes in the Southeast. Muscadines are the most naturally disease-resistant grapes for the Gulf South — they have evolved in the region and require far fewer pesticide applications than bunch grapes in the humid Southeast. Jumbo is perfect for fresh eating, home juice, wine, and preserves. Train on a 5–6 foot high wire trellis and prune hard in winter, leaving only 2–4 lateral spurs per cane.

common_name: Summit Muscadine (Large Bronze Self-Fertile Muscadine Grape)

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root vines in late winter or spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun on a sturdy trellis or arbor; Summit is self-fertile and particularly well-suited for fresh eating due to its large berry size and mild, sweet flavor.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun on a sturdy trellis or arbor; Summit is a bronze (amber-green) muscadine grape cultivar released by the University of Georgia — selected for its combination of large berry size, excellent fresh-eating quality, self-fertility, and late-season ripening. Bronze muscadines are generally milder and sweeter in flavor than black muscadines, making Summit particularly outstanding for fresh table use, wine production (bronze muscadines produce white/rosé wines), and juice. The large, uniform berries ripen in mid-to-late season (September in zone 9) in loose clusters that fall into collection bags or tarps for easy harvesting — a distinct muscadine characteristic compared to bunch grapes. Summit's self-fertility makes it an excellent standalone vine for gardeners with limited space, though yields are typically improved with a male or other self-fertile pollinizer within 50 feet. Muscadines are the native grapes of the southeastern United States — naturally adapted to Gulf South heat, humidity, and disease pressure that make European (Vitis vinifera) grapes impractical without intensive spray programs. Summit is disease-resistant, long-lived, and productive — established vines 20–30 years old commonly produce 30–50 pounds of fruit per year. The vine becomes a significant landscape feature as it ages, with thick, gnarled main canes and attractive fall foliage.
